entity: North Carolina Committee for Interracial Cooperation
explicit_text: I first got involved with the North Carolina Committee for Interracial Cooperation back in 1924, shortly after I moved here. The organization was based in Raleigh, though it never had a full-time secretary due to limited funds. We worked closely with the Virginia Commission for Interracial Cooperation for some years, sharing a secretary named L.R. Reynolds. The committee held annual meetings where the governor often spoke, lending official sanction to interracial cooperation efforts during a time when such meetings were not widely accepted.
implicit_text: I first got involved with the state-level interracial cooperation committee back in 1924, shortly after I moved here. The organization was based in the state capital, though it never had a full-time secretary due to limited funds. We worked closely with the neighboring state's interracial commission for some years, sharing a secretary named L.R. Reynolds. The committee held annual meetings where the governor often spoke, lending official sanction to interracial cooperation efforts during a time when such meetings were not widely accepted.

explicit_text: After the North Carolina Committee for Interracial Cooperation evolved and eventually disbanded, I became involved with the Southern Regional Council. This transition marked a new phase in interracial cooperation work in the South. My experience with the earlier commission helped inform my role in the Council, especially as we sought to build on past efforts and address racial issues more effectively across the region.
implicit_text: After the state interracial cooperation committee evolved and eventually disbanded, I became involved with the regional council focused on southern interracial cooperation. This transition marked a new phase in interracial cooperation work in the South. My experience with the earlier commission helped inform my role in the council, especially as we sought to build on past efforts and address racial issues more effectively across the region.

explicit_text: Raleigh was the hub for the North Carolina Committee for Interracial Cooperation, where the state office was located. Although the office was modest and the secretary position part-time, Raleigh served as the central meeting place for annual gatherings. The governor of North Carolina would often deliver speeches at these meetings held in Raleigh, which helped give the interracial cooperation efforts a degree of official respectability.

explicit_text: The Virginia Commission for Interracial Cooperation worked closely with the North Carolina Committee for Interracial Cooperation for several years. We shared the services of L.R. Reynolds, who was based in Richmond but traveled to North Carolina occasionally. Despite this cooperation, the arrangement was not very effective, and eventually, North Carolina decided to end the partnership due to lack of attention and results.

explicit_text: L.R. Reynolds was the secretary shared by the North Carolina and Virginia Commissions for Interracial Cooperation. He was based in Richmond and traveled sporadically to North Carolina, but his efforts were limited and often ineffective. He maintained a large card index of contacts and reported on his travels at annual meetings, but he lacked organizational skills. Eventually, he moved to Texas and may have worked with the Texas Commission, though I am not sure what became of him.

explicit_text: Gurney Hood was a member of the state banking commission in North Carolina, and at one point, L.R. Reynolds used Hood's office address in Raleigh for correspondence. Hood's involvement with the Interracial Commission came later, not in the earliest days. His office served as a mailing address, but mail replies often sat there for a long time before being forwarded.

explicit_text: Dr. Alexander was one of the early thinkers behind the idea of organizing state commissions on interracial cooperation with some official backing. He helped persuade the governor of North Carolina to issue the call for the first meeting, which gave the commissions a degree of official sanction. His role was important in shaping the strategy to make interracial cooperation more respectable and acceptable at a time when such efforts faced resistance.

explicit_text: The governor of North Carolina played a key role in the establishment and support of the Interracial Commission by issuing invitations and calls for meetings. Governors like Cam Morrison in the early 1920s and later O. Max Gardner in the late 1920s lent their names and presence to annual meetings, which helped legitimize the interracial cooperation efforts. The governor's speeches at these events were public and helped attract larger audiences, giving the commission a measure of official respectability.

explicit_text: The General Education Board provided some special grants that made projects possible for the North Carolina Committee for Interracial Cooperation. These grants supported efforts like the bibliography of Negro materials and traveling libraries. Their funding helped facilitate educational and interracial cooperation initiatives during a time when state appropriations for Negro education were very limited.

explicit_text: N.C. Newbold was the State Director of Negro Education in North Carolina during segregation. He was a shy but sensible white man who acted as a spokesman for black education interests. He successfully pushed for increased appropriations for Negro schools and colleges and gained a national reputation for his work. Newbold also helped secure foundation grants that supported projects like the bibliography and interracial student meetings.

The bibliography was published to help librarians and Negro libraries build better collections on African American subjects.

entity: North Carolina Agricultural and Technical State University
explicit_text: North Carolina Agricultural and Technical State University, or A&T, was actively involved in the interracial student meetings organized under the auspices of the North Carolina Committee for Interracial Cooperation. Students from A&T joined peers from Duke, UNC, North Carolina Central, and Meredith College to discuss race relations and share experiences. Additionally, students from A&T helped craft a beautiful traveling library case for a project aimed at circulating books on Negro subjects to various schools.
